title: Echinacea root tincture for immune support
subtitle: A concentrated tincture taken at the first sign of a cold
excerpt: Echinacea purpurea root macerated in 40% vodka for four to six weeks. Taken in short courses at the first sign of a cold or infection. The most extensively studied traditional immune herb in western herbal medicine, with multiple systematic reviews supporting short-course use.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), echinacea entry, introduced to British herbalism from the American eclectic tradition. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), echinacea as a blood purifier and infection-fighter. WHO Monograph on Echinacea purpurea (1999), immune stimulation, clinical evidence overview. EMA HMPC monograph on Echinaceae purpureae radix (2015), confirms traditional use for prevention and treatment of common cold symptoms.

content.text: Echinacea root packed into a jar with vodka, left to steep for four to six weeks, then strained and bottled for short-course use at the first sign of infection. Echinacea is the most studied traditional

in western herbalism. Dozens of clinical trials back its use for cutting short the common cold when started early. The tincture is the strongest household form. It pulls out the alkamides, the constituents behind the immune-stimulating action, into a form the body can take up quickly.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Daisy-family allergy: echinacea is in the daisy family. If you react to ragweed, chamomile, or marigold, patch-test before internal use.

Immune-suppressing drugs: echinacea is an immune stimulant. Do not use it alongside immune-suppressing drugs (used after organ transplants, for autoimmune conditions, or for some cancers) without medical advice. The two may work against each other.

Autoimmune conditions: get specialist advice before using echinacea if you have rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, MS, or similar.

Course length: use in short courses of 7 to 10 days at most. Long-running use is not recommended and may reduce the effect. Take a break of at least two weeks between courses.

Pregnancy: data on echinacea in pregnancy is limited. One cohort study found no extra risk at usual doses in the first trimester. The traditional guidance is to ask a herbalist or your midwife before using echinacea in pregnancy.

Alcohol content: each 2 ml dose holds about 0.8 ml of vodka, roughly 0.7 units.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Dried echinacea root
items.prepNote: dried Echinacea purpurea root, cut and sifted

content.text: Place {{dried-echinacea-root}} of dried echinacea root in the steeping jar. Pour {{vodka-40-percent}} of vodka over. The spirit should cover the root completely, with at least 1 cm above the top of the herb. Seal tightly and label with the date. Shake the jar daily for the first week.
Macerate 4, 6 weeks, then press and strain

After 4 to 6 weeks in a cool, dark place, strain the contents through muslin-lined sieve into a jug. Gather the muslin and press firmly to extract all remaining liquid. The finished tincture will be dark amber-brown with a slightly peppery, aromatic taste, echinacea root has a distinctive tingling sensation on the tongue, which is normal and indicates the alkamide content. Decant into amber dropper bottles.

Adults: 2, 4 ml (40, 80 drops) taken in a small amount of water, up to three times daily, for a maximum of 7 to 10 days per course. Start at the first sign of a cold or infection. Take a break of at least two weeks between courses. Do not take continuously. For prevention during exposure to infection, 2 ml twice daily for up to ten days is within the traditional range.
Finished tincture keeps 2 years in a sealed amber bottle at room temperature away from light and heat.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not use with immunosuppressant medication. Discuss with prescriber if you have an autoimmune condition. Avoid if you have an Asteraceae allergy. Do not use for more than 10 consecutive days, always take a break between courses. See your doctor for infections that are worsening rather than improving, for fever above 38.5°C that persists beyond 48 hours, or for any infection in an immunocompromised person.
